title: Angkor Enterprise Launches Online Ticketing System to Boost Tourism - Construction & Property News
highlights: visitors, Angkor Enterprise has officially launched a comprehensive online ticket sales and inspection system for Angkor. The initiative, which seeks to attract tourists from around the globe, was inaugurated on 30 January 2024, under the leadership of HE Deputy Prime Minister Neth Savoeun, representing His Majesty King Norodom Sihamoni. The newly modernised online ticketing system allows international tourists to conveniently purchase entry tickets through the Angkor website and via a dedicated mobile app available on both Google and Apple platforms. This effort aligns with the ongoing evolution of information technology, ensuring a seamless experience for travellers and tour  ...    and The Heritage Walk with three. In total, 15 self-service machines have been installed to expedite ticket purchasing. title: Gov’t to Take Full Control of Angkor Wat, Sokimex Pushed Out - Khmer Times
highlights: The government will take full control of the iconic Angkor Wat temple complex from Sokimex Group, which had been authorized to manage the complex for more than 20 years, Prime Minister Hun Sen announced this morning in a post on his  ...    Sokimex received a concession to manage the temple complex in April 1999. The conglomerate, which runs fuel stations and hotels, is owned by tycoon Sok Kong. The controversial concession allowed his company to collect fees from ticket sales and share part of the proceeds with the government. The government will form a new public enterprise to manage Angkor Wat that will comprise officials from the Ministry of  ...    A report from the Apsara Authority, the government agency that manages the site, showed revenue from ticket sales at Angkor Wat rose 0.62 percent in the first 10 months of the year, to slightly more than $47 million. title: Sokimex and Government revisit Angkor deal | The Phnom Penh Post
highlights:
Cambodian Government has hammered out a new agreement with private company Sokimex over the ticket sales to the famed Angkor temples near Siem Reap. The agreement, reached at an interministerial meeting on Aug 15, may bring higher revenue to the Government. A Government official says Sokimex agreed to hand over 50 percent of its income if ticket sales to the Angkor park are lower than $3 million,  ...    Currently Sokimex pays the Government a flat fee of $1 million a year and keeps all

revenue from ticket sales.  ...    Sokimex was granted the concession to ticket sales to the sprawling 9th-to-15th century temple complex, Cambodia's biggest tourism draw, last year. ## About Us Angkor Enterprise is a public administration, which is under the technical supervision of the Ministry of Tourism, and is under the financial supervision of the Ministry of Economy and Finance and has the following duties: Manage and collect revenue from selling Angkor Archaeological Park ticket Provide a proposal on how to collect revenue of selling ticket from Angkor Archaeological Park Participate in the state budget
